title: Black Box Data After a Semi Crash in Austin, Texas

If you or someone you know has been involved in a semi-truck crash in Austin, Texas, one of the most overlooked yet critical steps is preserving the truck's black box data — also known as the ECM (Engine Control Module) or EDR (Event Data Recorder).

This onboard technology records vehicle speed, brake application, throttle position, hours of service, and more in the moments before a collision. In major truck accident litigation, this data can make or break a case. The problem? Trucking companies and their insurers move fast. Black box data can be overwritten, lost, or deliberately destroyed if a legal hold isn't issued immediately.

facebook:
🚛 Were you or a loved one hit by a semi-truck in Austin, Texas?

Here's something most people don't know: every commercial truck on the road carries a black box — similar to what you'd find on an airplane. After a crash, that device records speed, braking, engine data, and more. It can be the single most powerful piece of evidence in your case.

But here's the catch — that data doesn't last forever. Trucking companies and their insurance carriers act fast after an accident, and if no one steps in to preserve that evidence, it can be overwritten or lost for good.

You deserve to know the truth about what happened to you. An experienced truck accident attorney can send an immediate legal hold to protect that data before it disappears.

WreckMatch connects injured victims in Austin with attorneys who specialize in exactly these cases — at no cost to you.

Have you ever wondered what information a truck's black box actually captures — and how it could change the outcome of an accident claim? 👇
reddit_title: Semi-truck crash in Texas — does the truck's black box data actually matter for your injury claim, and how long before it's gone?
reddit_body:
If you've been in a crash involving a commercial semi-truck in Texas, one of the most important things to understand is that the truck itself was recording data the entire time. The ECM (Engine Control Module) and EDR (Event Data Recorder) log vehicle speed, brake activity, throttle input, engine RPMs, and sometimes hours-of-service records. In a serious accident, this information can directly contradict what a driver or trucking company claims happened.

The urgent issue is preservation. Unlike personal injury cases involving regular passenger vehicles, trucking cases move on a tighter timeline. ECM data can be overwritten during normal truck operations within days. Once it's gone, it's gone. An attorney experienced in commercial trucking accidents will typically issue a spoliation letter immediately — a formal legal notice demanding the trucking company preserve all data, dashcam footage, maintenance records, and driver logs.

If you're trying to figure out your next step after a truck accident in Texas, the most important thing you can do is contact a qualified truck accident attorney as soon as possible — not a general personal injury lawyer, but someone who specifically handles commercial carrier cases. A free matching service like wreckmatch.com can connect you with attorneys in their network who handle exactly these cases, so you're not wasting time calling firms that don't have the right experience.
